title: A Tale of Two Strategic Attacks: The Race to End the War in Ukraine
gist: Ukraine and Russia are racing two attritional deep-strike campaigns — Ukraine's drone assault on Russian oil/gas infrastructure versus Russia's ballistic-missile punishment campaign, whose collapsing 10.2% interception rate is the lever most likely to force a negotiated settlement within six months.

insights.claim: The war in Ukraine has become a race between two competing attritional theories of victory: Ukraine's deep strikes on Russian energy infrastructure to fracture elite/economic support for the Kremlin, versus Russia's punishment campaign against Ukrainian infrastructure and cities to force a settlement on Kremlin terms — and these dueling strike campaigns, not the ground front, are most likely to force a settlement within six months.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Watch strike-campaign metrics (energy strike volume, missile interception rates), not front-line territory, as the leading indicator of when/how the war ends.
insights.quote: these dueling deep strike campaigns are the lines of effort most likely to force a settlement over the next six months

insights.claim: Ukraine's air and drone strikes inside Russia rose from about 580 in 2023 to 7,244 in 2024, with 901 of the 2024 strikes aimed at energy infrastructure (fuel storage, oil depots, refineries, pipelines); strikes in just the first half of 2025 (6,176) suggest the full-year 2025 total may have nearly doubled 2024's.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The strike tempo against Russian energy assets is accelerating sharply, not plateauing — a rising, not stable, pressure on Russian oil revenue.
insights.locator: Ukraine's launches of air and drone strikes in Russia have increased significantly

insights.claim: Oil and gas taxes and export tariffs comprised 45 percent of Russia's federal budget revenue just before the full-scale invasion, which is the structural rationale for Ukraine targeting refineries, export terminals and storage facilities well behind the front line.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Quantifies why energy-infrastructure strikes are Ukraine's most economically leveraged lever against the Russian state, independent of battlefield outcomes.
insights.locator: Russia suffers from these attacks due to its dependence on oil and gas

insights.claim: Ukraine has extended its strike campaign to maritime energy logistics, using aerial and Sea Baby drones against tankers in Russia's shadow fleet; a wave of strikes on 90 Russian vessels led to the suspension of shipping in the Sea of Azov.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Signals a widening front against Russian energy exports beyond fixed infrastructure — relevant to shadow-fleet insurance, shipping and sanctions-evasion risk.
insights.locator: Ukraine has also extended its campaign from fixed infrastructure to maritime energy logistics

themes.theme: Russia's punishment campaign and the missile-defense gap

insights.claim: Russia's firepower strategy uses large salvos of ballistic missiles and drones against critical infrastructure to substitute for a stalled ground offensive, aiming to exhaust Ukrainian air defenses and inflict psychological strain that could force premature Ukrainian concessions.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Russia's theory of victory runs through civilian/political resolve, not ground gains — reframes what "winning" looks like for the Kremlin.
insights.locator: Russia's firepower strike strategy operates as a deliberate punishment campaign

insights.claim: Ukraine intercepts long-range one-way attack drones like the Shahed more than 85 percent of the time, but ballistic missile defense has deteriorated sharply: Russia increased ballistic missile launches (and by extension production capacity) over 700 percent between 2023 and 2025, while Ukraine's ballistic intercept rate fell 36 percent and now stands at only 10.2 percent in 2026, a drop of over 200 percent since the start of the war.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Ballistic missile defense, not drone defense, is the critical vulnerability to track — it is the metric most likely to drive Ukrainian leadership toward a negotiated off-ramp.

insights.claim: Russia has fired 167 ballistic missiles at Ukraine since January 1, 2026; if the historical pattern of ramping launches toward year-end holds, Moscow is on track to fire up to 840 ballistic missiles in 2026, and at the current 10.2 percent interception rate that would let roughly 754 missiles reach their targets.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable forecast — watch monthly ballistic launch counts against this 840 trajectory as a leading indicator of escalation risk into year-end.
insights.locator: Moscow is on track to fire up to 840 ballistic missiles in 2026

themes.theme: How allies can shift the balance

insights.claim: Russia's 20th Directorate, a military intelligence unit, buys foreign electronics through shell companies and lax-jurisdiction customs gaps to sustain the long-range strike campaign; the US and allies should declassify and share actionable intelligence on these front operations and expand Treasury secondary sanctions against the third-country intermediaries and financial institutions involved.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific sanctions-enforcement gap (component procurement via shell companies) that, if closed, would directly constrain Russian missile production capacity.

insights.claim: International partners should redirect intelligence sharing toward high-priority ballistic-missile-source targets — mobile Iskander launchers, deep manufacturing plants, and rocket-motor production facilities — to disrupt missiles before they enter the logistics pipeline, reducing the burden on Ukraine's strained air defense network.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Argues for shifting Ukrainian long-range strike allocation from interception to left-of-launch disruption — a resource-allocation thesis for Ukraine's strike planners.

insights.claim: In 2024, Germany, Spain, Romania and the Netherlands signed an almost $6 billion contract to manufacture Patriot PAC-2 interceptors at a new MBDA facility in southern Germany, and at the July 2026 NATO summit US Under Secretary of Defense Michael Duffey signaled willingness to explore additional European coproduction; European states should tap European Investment Bank Group funding to accelerate additional PAC-2 production lines and transfer more interceptors to Ukraine.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Interceptor supply, not just missile-source disruption, is a bottleneck with a concrete near-term financing solution (EIB funding for PAC-2 lines) that could materially raise Ukraine's intercept rate faster than domestic Ukrainian Patriot licensing, which could take years.
